topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with a dull thud, instantly extinguishing the festive glow I’d been cultivating. "Due to unforeseen circumstances, the surprise party scheduled for Saturday has been cancelled." My thumb hovered over the screen, the message blurring slightly. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, spreading outwards like ink in water. I reread the message three times, searching for a misplaced comma, a typo that would somehow invalidate the news. Silence stretched in the room; the cheerful music I’d been planning to play seemed suddenly deafening. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the living room, my hands flexing at my sides. Every shadow in the hallway seemed to deepen, and my breath hitched in shallow, quick bursts. I needed to call someone, anyone, just to confirm, to anchor myself against this sudden void.

[story 2]

I stared at the text message, the screen illuminating my face in a sickly pale light. *Cancelled.* The single word echoed in the sudden stillness of my apartment. My heart began to hammer against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm that drowned out the distant city sounds. I dropped the phone onto the worn armchair as if it had burned me. I walked over to the window, peering out at the mundane street below, unable to focus on anything. My jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. A wave of nausea washed over me, making the air feel thick and hard to draw in. I began to scrub at my face with the back of my hand, a desperate attempt to clear the fog that had suddenly descended.

[story 3]

When I finally read the confirmation—a brief, impersonal notification—the world seemed to tilt on its axis. The carefully constructed excitement I had nurtured for weeks evaporated, leaving behind a hollow, echoing emptiness. I sank onto the floor, curling into a tight ball, pulling my knees up to my chest. A tremor ran through my limbs, making it difficult to stay still. My gaze drifted to the decorations still haphazardly strewn around the room—balloons deflated, streamers drooping sadly. I let out a slow, shaky exhale, the sound thin and reedy. I started picking at a loose thread on the rug, my movements jerky and uncoordinated, a physical manifestation of the unsettled feeling churning deep within my core.
